Hi Greg. Hi, I'm Paulo. I'm new mb. Hi Paul, I'm Ka Carter. I'm a list MP based in Otto Tahi Christchurch. Hi. Alright, well thanks Joshua. Um, you can take your submission as read, but obviously you would like to speak to the highlights of it.

Um, we have 10 minutes and hopefully time for questions at the end. Yeah, it'll take about six and a half minutes. Um, yeah, so I'll just briefly review the submission. You probably already read it, um, but I'll take a bit of time to respond to the Department of Internal Affairs, uh, response to our petition. Um, as you mentioned, I'm Joshua O'Reilly.

I'm here on behalf of Sovereign and thank you for the opportunity to, uh, speak to our petition at its heart. Our petition addresses a fundamental inconsistency and our democracy. We currently operate a two-tiered system of electoral transparency in New Zealand. Our national elections are held to one standard of oversight, while our local elections are held to a lower standard, our petition as for one simple and powerful change, and that is to repeal section 81 1 of the local electoral act.

This would end the ban on Scrutineers, observing the early processing of, uh, local election ballots, and finally align our local elections with higher transparency standards that we rightly enjoy from our national elections. The current law creates a profound contradiction. Section 80 allows electoral officers to begin processing ballots weeks before polling closes.

But section 81 explicitly bans the very people who act as the public's eyes and ears. That is the candidate's scrutinies, uh, from watching this happen. This is a, a minor, uh, this isn't a minor procedural issue affecting just a handful of votes. As our research shows in the 2022 elections, up to 90% of all ballots in major council areas like Christchurch and Auckland were processed before the scrutinies were allowed in the room.

This means that the ma vast majority of our local democratic process takes place behind closed doors, uh, creating a significant democratic deficit. When the handling of nine out of every 10 votes is hidden from independent partisan oversight, public confidence is inevitably eroded. Our petition offers a simple and proven, uh, no cost solution that is to adopt the model already used for national elections where scrutiny or oversight is a cornerstone of integrity.

The Department of Internal Affairs has submitted in defense of the current status quo. However, their arguments are unconvincing and are based on flawed reasoning and fundamental misunderstanding of both risk and process. I will adjust their four main points. First, the department argues that the presence of a JP provides adequate independent scrutiny.

This represents a fundamental misunderstanding of the different roles. A JP is a passive observer appointed by the electoral officer to certify that a procedure was followed. Their role is clerical. A a scrutiny in contrast is an active adversarial representative of a candidate. Their job is to be skeptical, to question ambiguous ballots and to defend their candidates interests.

The DIA itself, uh, quotes the electoral commission, which states that party scrutinies provide independent assurance in general elections, uh, a JP is appointed by the very official they are meant to be observing and simply cannot provide the same level of robust adversarial scrutiny that builds, uh, true public trust.

And second, the department raises the fear that Scrutinies might observe, uh, observe and leak voting trends, uh, improperly influencing an election that is still underway. This concern is unfounded for two critical reasons. First, this is managed this, this risk is managed in our national elections, uh, secure, sorry, I'm getting over.

Flu secure, um, restricted areas and clear penalties for breaches work perfectly well. There is no reason for this successful model, uh, that this successful model cannot be applied to local elections. But more importantly, the DIA's fear is based on a technical impossibility.

The law makes a clear distinction between processing and counting votes. During early processing, a scrutiny was the administrative task of opening envelopes, validating individual ballots and scanning them. They would not see running totals, aggregated data or party vote shares. There is no statistically meaningful trend to observe, let alone to leak this objection, which is the DIA's primary argument is entirely baseless.

Third, the department suggests that modern automated voters voting systems make scrutinies and I quote, outdated and irrelevant. This argument, uh, which they source from private election companies themselves, is completely backward, opaque, centralized, and automated. Black box systems demand more independent human oversight, not less.

The less the public can see of the mechanics, the more they need trusted human representatives in the room to verify the integrity of the process. The DIA relied heavily on feedback from the two private entities, which process votes and therefore would benefit from removing the overhead associated with accommodating scrutineers.

The argument that there has been little interest in utilizing scrutineers is partly a direct result of section 81 1. For example, in the 2022 local elections, we had several scrutineers who had committed to travel to Auckland to oversee ballot processing. Upon discovering that only 20% of ballot processing was subject to scrutiny, our scrutiny stood down.

Finally, the DIA suggest that we delay action on this specific law flaw and wait for a a wider future review of electoral processes. This is a classic diver diversionary tactic. We have identified a clear and serious problem that undermines public trust. We have also proposed a simple specific solution that is already working successfully at the national level to delay a fix is to allow this democratic deficit to fester through another election cycle.

The argument to maintain the status quo does not hold up the DIA relies on false equivalence between the JP and scrutinous. A speculative risk that is both manageable and technically impossible. And a baffling argument that technolo technological opacity should be met with less transparency. The desire to process votes early does not remove the need to subject all ballot processing to the same level of scrutiny applied to general election ballots.

Public confidence is the bedrock of a healthy democracy. The current law actively damages the confidence by hiding up to 90 per 90% of the vote handling process. From the public view, we urge this committee to see past the bureaucracy's defense of a flawed system. We ask you to recommend that parliament repeal section 81 1 and restore the principles of transparency, accountability, and public trust to our local elections.
